beats.text: When outcomes are countable, a probability mass function assigns each outcome a probability, and those masses sum to one.
The atom of this whole section is the Bernoulli trial: one event scored one for success with probability p, zero otherwise.
The Binomial counts successes across n independent Bernoulli flips, with mean n times p and variance n times p times one minus p.

visual.note: A Binomial is just a sum of n Bernoullis

beats.text: Push the Binomial to many tiny-probability trials with fixed expected count lambda, and you get the Poisson law of rare events.
The Poisson signature is that its mean and variance are both lambda, so variance far above the mean signals an over-dispersed model.
The Geometric is the memoryless waiting time for the first success, taking one over p flips on average.

visual.label: average flips to first success at p = 0.01

concepts.title: Continuous distributions: spreading mass over a line

beats.text: When outcomes form a continuum, no single point carries positive probability, so we describe them with a probability density function.

visual.sub: Probability as area under a density

beats.text: For a density, probability is area under the curve between two points, and the total area always equals one.

visual.note: total area under f is one

beats.text: The Uniform is flat: every value in the interval is equally likely, and it is the seed every generator builds other distributions from.
The Normal is the bell curve, fixed by its mean and standard deviation, with symmetric and rapidly decaying tails.

visual.note: 68 to 95 to 99.7 within one, two, three sigma

beats.text: The Exponential is the continuous waiting time between Poisson events and is the only memoryless continuous distribution.
A unit uniform draw has variance one over twelve, the exact number the Central Limit Theorem grows a Normal from.

concepts.title: Moments: four numbers that describe a shape

beats.text: Four summary numbers called moments capture a distribution's location, spread, lopsidedness, and tail-heaviness.

visual.sub: Four numbers that describe a shape

beats.text: Mean is the centre of mass and variance is the average squared distance from it, with its root the standard deviation.
Skewness is the standardised third moment: zero for symmetric shapes, positive when the right tail is longer.
Kurtosis measures tail mass; the Normal has kurtosis three, so practitioners quote excess kurtosis relative to it.
Higher moments are estimated far less reliably than lower ones, dominated by the few most extreme points observed.

visual.label: excess kurtosis of Student-t at nu = 5

beats.text: For some heavy-tailed distributions the higher moments are infinite, so treat sample kurtosis as a hint, not a measurement.

concepts.title: The Central Limit Theorem: why the Normal is everywhere

beats.text: Take any distribution with finite mean and variance, average n independent samples, and the result converges to a Normal.
Recentred and rescaled, the sample mean converges in distribution to a standard Normal regardless of the parent shape.

visual.note: as n grows, any finite-variance parent

beats.text: Averaging just thirty flat Uniform draws already produces a clean bell, a parent shape that started perfectly flat.
The standard error falls like one over the square root of n, so halving uncertainty requires quadrupling the sample.
Convergence is fast for symmetric parents but slow for skewed ones, where you may need thirty to fifty draws.
The bell appears unbidden because any sum of many small independent contributions is approximately Normal by construction.

concepts.title: Heavy tails for quants: when the Normal lies

beats.text: The Central Limit Theorem requires finite variance, and real processes like financial returns break extreme far more often than a Normal allows.
The Student-t looks Normal in the middle but decays polynomially in the tails, governed by its degrees of freedom.

visual.note: tail decays like x to the minus nu plus one

beats.text: For daily and weekly returns a Student-t with three to six degrees of freedom typically fits; below four its kurtosis is infinite.

visual.label: degrees of freedom below which kurtosis is infinite

beats.text: Power laws have the heaviest tails of all, decaying only polynomially so the variance or even the mean can fail to exist.

visual.note: wealth, city sizes, word frequencies, catastrophic losses

beats.text: A six-sigma daily move is once in a million years under a Normal, yet happens every few years in real markets.
How fat the tails really are stays genuinely contested, so stress-test against several tail models and never trust one assumption.
